era_context: The year following Rome's decisive victory at Pydna, marking the consolidation of Roman hegemony over the Mediterranean. The Seleucid Empire is weakening under Antiochus IV's aggressive but ultimately unsustainable policies. Judea is in open revolt against Seleucid rule. Han China under Emperor Wen continues its reign. This is a pivotal year for the transition from Hellenistic to Roman political dominance and for the emergence of Jewish independence movements.

events.title: Maccabean Revolt: Mattathias and Judas Maccabeus lead Jewish resistance
events.description: In response to Antiochus IV's persecution and desecration of the Temple, Mattathias of Modein, an elderly Jewish priest, initiated armed resistance in 167 BCE. After Mattathias's death (likely in 166 BCE), his son Judas Maccabeus took command of the revolt. The Maccabees, a guerrilla force, defeated several Seleucid armies in pitched battles, including victories at Beth-horon and Emmaus. The revolt was driven by religious conviction and the desire for Jewish autonomy. By 164 BCE, the Maccabees had recaptured Jerusalem and rededicated the Temple, an event commemorated in the Jewish holiday of Hanukkah. The revolt would ultimately lead to Jewish independence under the Hasmonean dynasty.
events.certainty_note: The Maccabean Revolt is well-attested in Jewish sources, though these were written centuries after the events. The broad outline is accepted by modern scholarship, but specific dates and details of individual battles are sometimes uncertain. The revolt's religious motivation is clear from the sources.

events.title: Macedonian reorganization: Division into four republics
events.description: Following the Roman victory at Pydna in 168 BCE, the Roman Senate decided to reorganize Macedon to prevent the re-emergence of a unified Macedonian state that might challenge Rome. In 167 BCE, Macedon was divided into four separate republics, each with its own government and prohibited from intermarriage or trade with the others. This fragmentation was a deliberate Roman strategy to maintain hegemony. The division lasted until 148 BCE, when Macedon was formally annexed as a Roman province.
events.certainty_note: The reorganization of Macedon is well-documented in Polybius and Livy. The date and structure of the division are clearly attested.

events.title: Roman expulsion of Antiochus IV's envoys and assertion of hegemony
events.description: In 167 BCE, Rome sent a clear message to Antiochus IV by expelling his envoys and asserting its authority over Eastern Mediterranean affairs. This diplomatic action reinforced Rome's position as the supreme power in the region and signaled that Antiochus's aggressive policies would not be tolerated. The action was part of Rome's broader strategy to establish a hegemonic system in which Hellenistic kingdoms operated under Roman oversight.
events.certainty_note: The general fact of Roman assertion of hegemony over the Eastern Mediterranean in this period is well-established. Specific diplomatic incidents in 167 BCE are less clearly documented.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Continued stability under Emperor Wen
events.description: Emperor Wen of Han continued his reign, characterized by relative peace, economic development, and cautious foreign policy. The Han Dynasty during this period was consolidating its administrative systems and recovering from the civil conflicts of the early Han period. The reign of Emperor Wen (180–157 BCE) is remembered as a period of stability and the beginning of Han prosperity, though it lacked the military expansionism that would characterize the later reign of Emperor Wu.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The reign of Emperor Wen is well-documented in Chinese historical sources. The general characteristics of his rule are well-established, though specific events in 167 BCE are not clearly delineated.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating of the Maccabean Revolt's beginning is debated. Some scholars place Mattathias's initial resistance in 166 BCE rather than 167 BCE. The sequence of early battles and the role of Judas Maccabeus versus Mattathias is also somewhat unclear due to the later compilation of the sources. Additionally, some modern scholars argue that the Maccabean Revolt was as much a civil conflict within Jewish society (between Hellenized and traditional factions) as it was a resistance movement against Seleucid rule, a nuance that the later Jewish sources downplay.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 167 BCE is dominated by Mediterranean sources, particularly Greek and Roman accounts. Jewish sources provide crucial information on the Maccabean Revolt but were written centuries later and contain theological and legendary elements. Chinese sources are sparse for this year. The bias toward elite political and military history is pronounced; the experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite populations are largely absent from surviving records.

graph_edges.note: The Maccabean Revolt was triggered by Antiochus IV's persecution and desecration of the Temple
The division of Macedon was a consequence of Rome's victory at Pydna
The Maccabean Revolt led to the rededication of the Temple in 164 BCE
